title.raw: 卷一上 光武帝紀 Volume 1a: Annals of Emperor Guangwu

sentences.zh: 世祖光武皇帝諱秀，字文叔，

translations.literal: The Shizu Guangwu Emperor, taboo name Xiu, courtesy name Wenshu,
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu Shizu, whose given name was Xiu and courtesy name Wenshu,

sentences.zh: 〈禮「祖有功而宗有德」，光武中興，故廟稱世祖。

translations.literal: <The Rites say: 'Zu has merit and Zong has virtue.' Guangwu restored the dynasty, therefore his temple is called Shizu.
translations.idiomatic: <According to the Rites: 'Ancestors with merit are zu, those with virtue are zong.' Guangwu restored the Han dynasty, hence his temple name is Shizu.

sentences.zh: 謚法：「能紹前業曰光，克定禍亂曰武。

translations.literal: The system of posthumous names: 'Able to continue former enterprises is called Guang, able to settle calamities and disorders is called Wu.'
translations.idiomatic: According to the rules for posthumous titles: 'One who carries on past achievements is called Guang, one who overcomes chaos and calamity is called Wu.'

sentences.zh: 」伏侯《古今注》曰：「秀之字曰茂。

translations.literal: > Fu Hou's 'Notes on Ancient and Modern' says: 'Xiu's courtesy name was Mao.'
translations.idiomatic: > In Fu Hou's Notes on Ancient and Modern Affairs it says: 'Xiu's courtesy name was Mao.'

sentences.zh: 伯、仲、叔、季，兄弟之次。

translations.literal: Bo, Zhong, Shu, Ji are the order of brothers.
translations.idiomatic: Bo, Zhong, Shu, and Ji represent the birth order of brothers.

sentences.zh: 長兄伯升，次仲，故字文叔焉。」〉

translations.literal: The eldest brother is Bo Sheng, the second is Zhong, therefore his courtesy name is Wenshu.>
translations.idiomatic: The eldest brother was named Bo Sheng, the second was Zhong, hence his courtesy name Wenshu.>

sentences.zh: 南陽蔡陽人，

translations.literal: A man of Caiyang in Nanyang commandery,
translations.idiomatic: Native of Caiyang county in Nanyang commandery,

sentences.zh: 〈南陽，郡，今鄧州縣也。

translations.literal: <Nanyang is a commandery, now Dengzhou county.
translations.idiomatic: <Nanyang was a commandery, now equivalent to Dengzhou county.

sentences.zh: 蔡陽，縣，故城在今隨州棗陽縣西南。〉

translations.literal: Caiyang is a county, its old city is southwest of Zaoyang county in Suizhou today.>
translations.idiomatic: Caiyang was a county; its ancient city lies southwest of present-day Zaoyang county in Suizhou.>

sentences.zh: 高祖九世之孫也，出自景帝生長沙定王發。

translations.literal: He was the ninth-generation grandson of Gaozu, descended from Jingdi's son Changsha Dingwang Fa.
translations.idiomatic: He was Gaozu's ninth-generation descendant through Jingdi's son, King Ding of Changsha, named Fa.

sentences.zh: 〈長沙，郡，今潭州縣也。〉

translations.literal: <Changsha is a commandery, now Tanzhou county.>
translations.idiomatic: <Changsha was a commandery, now equivalent to Tanzhou county.>

sentences.zh: 發生舂陵節侯買，

translations.literal: Fa begat Chunling Jiehou Mai,
translations.idiomatic: Fa was the father of Jiehou Mai of Chunling,

sentences.zh: 〈舂陵，郷名，本屬零陵泠道縣，在今永州唐興縣北，元帝時徙南陽，仍號舂陵，故城今在隨州棗陽縣東。

translations.literal: <Chunling is a village name, originally belonged to Lengdao county of Lingling commandery, north of Tangxing county in Yongzhou today. During Yuandi's time it was moved to Nanyang, still called Chunling, its old city is east of Zaoyang county in Suizhou today.
translations.idiomatic: <Chunling was a village name, originally part of Lengdao county in Lingling commandery, located north of present-day Tangxing county in Yongzhou. During Emperor Yuan's reign it was relocated to Nanyang but kept the name Chunling; its ancient site lies east of Zaoyang county in Suizhou today.

sentences.zh: 事具宗室四王傳。〉

translations.literal: The matter is detailed in the Biographies of the Four Imperial Princes of the Imperial Clan.>
translations.idiomatic: Full details appear in the 'Biographies of the Four Imperial Princes of the Imperial Clan.'>

sentences.zh: 買生鬱林太守外，

translations.literal: Mai begat Yulin Grand Administrator Wai,
translations.idiomatic: Mai fathered Wai, Grand Administrator of Yulin,

sentences.zh: 〈鬱林，郡，今郴州縣。

translations.literal: <Yulin is a commandery, now Chenzhou county.
translations.idiomatic: <Yulin was a commandery, now equivalent to Chenzhou county.

sentences.zh: 《前書》曰：「郡守，秦官。

translations.literal: The 'Former Book' says: 'Commandery governor is a Qin office.'
translations.idiomatic: According to the History of the Former Han: 'The commandery governor was an office established in the Qin dynasty.'

sentences.zh: 秩二千石。

translations.literal: Rank of 2,000 shi.
translations.idiomatic: With a salary rank of 2,000 shi.

sentences.zh: 景帝更名太守。」〉

translations.literal: Emperor Jing changed the name to Grand Administrator.>
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing renamed it Grand Administrator.>

sentences.zh: 外生鉅鹿都尉回，

translations.literal: Wai begat Julu Commandant Hui,
translations.idiomatic: Wai fathered Hui, Commandant of Julu,

sentences.zh: 〈鉅鹿，郡，今邢州縣也。

translations.literal: <Julu is a commandery, now Xingzhou county.
translations.idiomatic: <Julu was a commandery, now equivalent to Xingzhou county.

sentences.zh: 《前書》曰：「都尉，本郡尉，秦官也。

translations.literal: The 'Former Book' says: 'Commandant, originally commandery captain, is a Qin office.'
translations.idiomatic: According to the History of the Former Han: 'The commandant was originally the commandery captain, an office established in the Qin dynasty.'

sentences.zh: 掌佐守，典武職，秩比二千石。

translations.literal: He assists the governor, oversees military affairs, rank equivalent to 2,000 shi.
translations.idiomatic: He assists the governor in administration and oversees military matters, with a rank equivalent to 2,000 shi.

sentences.zh: 景帝更名都尉。」〉

translations.literal: Emperor Jing changed the name to Commandant.>
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing renamed it Commandant.>

sentences.zh: 回生南頓令欽，

translations.literal: Hui begat Nandun Magistrate Qin,
translations.idiomatic: Hui fathered Qin, Magistrate of Nandun,

sentences.zh: 〈南頓，縣，屬汝南郡，故城在今陳州項城縣西。

translations.literal: <Nandun is a county, belonging to Runan commandery, its old city is west of Xiangcheng county in Chenzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: <Nandun was a county under Runan commandery; its ancient city lies west of present-day Xiangcheng county in Chenzhou.

sentences.zh: 《前書》曰：「令、長，皆秦官也。

translations.literal: The 'Former Book' says: 'Magistrate and chief are both Qin offices.'
translations.idiomatic: According to the History of the Former Han: 'Both magistrates and county chiefs were offices established in the Qin dynasty.'

sentences.zh: 萬戶以上為令，秩千石至六百石；

translations.literal: Counties of 10,000 households and above have magistrates, rank 1,000 to 600 shi;
translations.idiomatic: Counties with 10,000 or more households appoint magistrates with salaries ranging from 1,000 to 600 shi;

sentences.zh: 不滿萬戶為長，秩五百石至三百石。」〉

translations.literal: Those under 10,000 households have chiefs, rank 500 to 300 shi.>
translations.idiomatic: Counties with fewer than 10,000 households appoint chiefs with salaries from 500 to 300 shi.>

sentences.zh: 欽生光武。

translations.literal: Qin begat Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Qin was the father of Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 光武年九歲而孤，養於叔父良。

translations.literal: Guangwu became an orphan at age nine and was raised by his uncle Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu lost his parents at age nine and was raised by his uncle Liang.

sentences.zh: 身長七尺三寸，美須眉，大口，隆準，日角。

translations.literal: His height was seven chi three cun, with beautiful beard and eyebrows, large mouth, prominent nose bridge, and sun-like forehead corners.
translations.idiomatic: He stood seven chi three cun tall, with handsome beard and eyebrows, a large mouth, prominent nose, and forehead corners shaped like the sun.

sentences.zh: 〈隆，高也。

translations.literal: <Long means high.
translations.idiomatic: <Long means high.

sentences.zh: 許負云：「鼻頭為準。

translations.literal: Xu Fu said: 'The nose tip is called zhun.'
translations.idiomatic: The physiognomist Xu Fu said: 'The tip of the nose is called zhun.'

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄尚書中候注云：「日角謂庭中骨起，狀如日。」〉

translations.literal: > Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Zhonghou chapter of the Shangshu says: 'Sun-like forehead corners means the bones in the forehead rise up, shaped like the sun.'>
translations.idiomatic: > Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Zhonghou omen in the Shangshu explains: 'Sun-like forehead corners refer to bones rising in the forehead, shaped like the sun.'>

sentences.zh: 性勤於稼穡，

translations.literal: By nature diligent in farming,
translations.idiomatic: He was naturally diligent in agricultural work,

sentences.zh: 〈種曰稼，斂曰穡。〉

translations.literal: <Planting is called jia, harvesting is called se.>
translations.idiomatic: <Sowing is called jia, gathering is called se.>

sentences.zh: 而兄伯升好俠養士，常非笑光武事田業，比之高祖兄仲。

translations.literal: But his elder brother Bo Sheng loved knight-errantry and supporting scholars, and often ridiculed Guangwu for engaging in farming, comparing him to Gaozu's brother Zhong.
translations.idiomatic: His elder brother Bo Sheng, however, loved chivalric deeds and patronizing wandering scholars, and frequently mocked Guangwu for his farming pursuits, likening him to Gaozu's brother Zhong.

sentences.zh: 〈仲，郃陽侯喜也，能為產業。

translations.literal: <Zhong was Marquis of Heyang Xi, skilled at managing property.
translations.idiomatic: <Zhong was Xi, Marquis of Heyang, who excelled at managing estates.

sentences.zh: 見《前書》。〉

translations.literal: See the Former Book.>
translations.idiomatic: See the History of the Former Han.>

sentences.zh: 王莽天鳳中，

translations.literal: During Wang Mang's Tianfeng period,
translations.idiomatic: During the Tianfeng era of Wang Mang's reign,

sentences.zh: 〈王莽建國六年改為天鳳。〉

translations.literal: <Wang Mang changed the name to Tianfeng in the sixth year of his Jian'guo reign.>
translations.idiomatic: <Wang Mang renamed it Tianfeng in the sixth year of his Jian'guo period.>

sentences.zh: 乃之長安，受尚書，略通大義。

translations.literal: He went to Chang'an to study the Shangshu and gained a general understanding of its great principles.
translations.idiomatic: He traveled to Chang'an to study the Classic of Documents, acquiring a broad grasp of its fundamental teachings.

sentences.zh: 〈《東觀記》曰：「受尚書於中大夫廬江許子威。

translations.literal: <The Dongguan Ji says: 'He studied the Shangshu under the Palace Attendant Xu Ziwei of Lujiang.'
translations.idiomatic: <The Dongguan Records state: 'He studied the Classic of Documents under Xu Ziwei, Palace Attendant of Lujiang.'

sentences.zh: 資用乏，與同舍生韓子合錢買驢，令從者僦，以給諸公費。」〉

translations.literal: Lacking funds, he pooled money with his dormitory mate Han Zi to buy a donkey, had his attendant rent it out, to provide for the expenses of the various gentlemen.>
translations.idiomatic: Short on resources, he combined funds with his roommate Han Zi to purchase a donkey, which his servant rented out to cover the costs for the various scholars.>

sentences.zh: 莽末，天下連歲災蝗，寇盜鋒起。

translations.literal: At the end of Wang Mang's reign, the empire suffered successive years of disasters and locusts, bandits arose in great numbers.
translations.idiomatic: Near the end of Wang Mang's rule, the realm endured years of continuous calamities and locust plagues, with bandits rising up everywhere like sharpened blades.

sentences.zh: 〈言賊鋒銳競起。

translations.literal: <Saying that bandits arose sharply in competition.
translations.idiomatic: <Meaning bandits arose with sharpened blades, competing with each other.

sentences.zh: 字或作「蜂」，諭多也。〉

translations.literal: The character is sometimes written as 'feng', meaning numerous.>
translations.idiomatic: The character is alternatively written as 'bee', conveying the sense of multitude.>

sentences.zh: 地皇三年，

translations.literal: In the third year of the Dihuang era,
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of the Dihuang reign period,

sentences.zh: 〈天鳳六年改為地皇。〉

translations.literal: <Changed from Tianfeng in the sixth year to Dihuang.>
translations.idiomatic: <Renamed from Tianfeng to Dihuang in the sixth year.>

sentences.zh: 南陽荒饑，

translations.literal: Nanyang suffered famine and starvation,
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang endured widespread famine,

sentences.zh: 〈《韓詩外傳》曰：「一穀不升曰歉，二穀不升曰饑，三穀不升曰饉，四穀不升曰荒，五穀不升曰大侵。」〉

translations.literal: <The Han Poetry Outer Commentary says: 'One grain not rising is called qian, two grains not rising is called ji, three grains not rising is called jin, four grains not rising is called huang, five grains not rising is called great invasion.'>
translations.idiomatic: <According to the Han Poetry Outer Commentary: 'Failure of one grain to ripen is called scarcity, two grains is famine, three grains is dearth, four grains is desolation, five grains is great calamity.'>

sentences.zh: 諸家賔客多為小盜。

translations.literal: The retainers of various families mostly became petty bandits.
translations.idiomatic: The retainers of many noble houses turned to minor banditry.

sentences.zh: 光武避吏新野，

translations.literal: Guangwu avoided officials in Xinyye,
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu evaded government officials by taking refuge in Xinyye,

sentences.zh: 〈新野屬南陽郡，今鄧州縣。

translations.literal: <Xinyye belonged to Nanyang commandery, now Dengzhou county.
translations.idiomatic: <Xinyye was under Nanyang commandery, now equivalent to Dengzhou county.

sentences.zh: 《續漢書》曰：「伯升賔客劫人，上避吏於新野鄧晨家。」〉

translations.literal: The Xu Han Shu says: 'Bo Sheng's retainers robbed people, the emperor avoided officials at Deng Chen's house in Xinyye.'>
translations.idiomatic: The Xu Han Shu records: 'Bo Sheng's retainers engaged in robbery, so the emperor took refuge from officials at Deng Chen's home in Xinyye.'>

sentences.zh: 因賣榖於宛。

translations.literal: And sold grain in Wan.
translations.idiomatic: Thereupon he sold grain in Wan.

sentences.zh: 〈《東觀記》曰：「時南陽旱饑，而上田獨收。

translations.literal: <The Dongguan Records say: 'At this time Nanyang suffered drought and famine, but the emperor's fields alone yielded a harvest.'
translations.idiomatic: <The Dongguan Records note: 'While Nanyang endured drought and famine at this time, only the emperor's fields produced a good harvest.'

sentences.zh: 」宛，縣，屬南陽郡，故城今鄧州南陽縣也。〉

translations.literal: >Wan is a county belonging to Nanyang commandery, its old city is now Nanyang county in Dengzhou.>
translations.idiomatic: >Wan was a county under Nanyang commandery; its ancient site is now Nanyang county in Dengzhou.>

sentences.zh: 宛人李通等以圖讖說光武云：「劉氏復起，李氏為輔。」

translations.literal: Li Tong and others from Wan persuaded Guangwu with prophetic charts saying: 'The Liu clan will rise again, the Li clan will assist.'
translations.idiomatic: Li Tong and other men of Wan persuaded Guangwu with prophetic charts, saying: 'The Liu clan will rise again, with the Li clan as their helpers.'

sentences.zh: 〈圖，河圖也。

translations.literal: <Tu means Hetu.
translations.idiomatic: <Tu refers to the Yellow River Diagram.

sentences.zh: 讖，符命之書。

translations.literal: Chen means books of prophetic omens.
translations.idiomatic: Chen refers to writings containing omens of heavenly mandate.

sentences.zh: 讖，驗也。

translations.literal: Chen means verification.
translations.idiomatic: Chen means verification or proof.

sentences.zh: 言為王者受命之徵驗也。

translations.literal: Meaning the verification that a king receives the Mandate of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Meaning the signs that verify a ruler has received the Mandate of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 《易坤靈圖》曰：「漢之臣李陽也。」〉

translations.literal: The Yi Kunling Tu says: 'Han minister Li Yang.'>
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes' Kun Spirit Diagram states: 'Li Yang is Han's minister.'>

sentences.zh: 光武初不敢當，然獨念兄伯升素結輕客，必舉大事，且王莽敗亡已兆，天下方亂，遂與定謀，於是乃市兵弩。

translations.literal: At first Guangwu dared not accept, but alone he thought of his brother Bo Sheng who had long associated with bold retainers and was sure to undertake great deeds, moreover Wang Mang's defeat and fall were already apparent, the realm was in chaos, so he made plans with them, thereupon he purchased weapons and crossbows.
translations.idiomatic: Initially Guangwu did not dare to accept the prophecy, but he reflected privately that his brother Bo Sheng had long cultivated ties with daring retainers and was certain to launch a major uprising. Moreover, signs of Wang Mang's defeat and downfall were already evident, and the empire was in turmoil. Thus he plotted with them and proceeded to purchase weapons and crossbows.

sentences.zh: 十月，與李通從弟軼等起於宛，時年二十八。

translations.literal: In the tenth month, with Li Tong's cousin Yi and others he rose up in Wan, at that time twenty-eight years old.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth month, together with Li Tong's cousin Yi and others, he rose in rebellion at Wan, at that time twenty-eight years old.

sentences.zh: 十一月，有星孛于張。

translations.literal: In the eleventh month, there was a comet in Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: In the eleventh month, a comet appeared in the constellation Zhang.

sentences.zh: 〈《前書》音義曰：「孛星光芒短，蓬然。

translations.literal: <The Former Book's Phonetic Explanations say: 'A comet has short rays, disheveled.'
translations.idiomatic: <According to the Former Han's phonetic glosses: 'A bo star has short rays that appear disheveled.'

sentences.zh: 張，南方宿也。

translations.literal: Zhang is the southern lodge.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang is the southern constellation.

sentences.zh: 」《續漢志》曰：「張為周地。

translations.literal: >The Xu Han Zhi says: 'Zhang is Zhou territory.'
translations.idiomatic: >The Xu Han Treatise on Astronomy states: 'Zhang corresponds to Zhou territory.'

sentences.zh: 星孛于張，東南行即翼、軫之分。

translations.literal: A comet in Zhang, traveling southeast to the boundary of Yi and Zhen.
translations.idiomatic: The comet appeared in Zhang and traveled southeast toward the region between Yi and Zhen constellations.

sentences.zh: 翼、軫，楚地，是楚地將有兵亂。

translations.literal: Yi and Zhen are Chu territory, this means Chu territory will have military disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Yi and Zhen correspond to Chu lands, signifying that military turmoil will arise in Chu territory.

sentences.zh: 後一年正月，光武起兵舂陵，攻南陽，斬阜、賜等，殺其士衆數萬人。

translations.literal: The following year in the first month, Guangwu raised troops at Chunling, attacked Nanyang, executed Fu, Ci and others, killing tens of thousands of their soldiers and people.
translations.idiomatic: The following year in the first month, Guangwu raised troops at Chunling, attacked Nanyang commandery, executed Fu, Ci, and their confederates, slaughtering tens of thousands of their soldiers and followers.

sentences.zh: 光武都雒陽，居周地，除穢布新之象。」〉

translations.literal: Guangwu established his capital at Luoyang, residing in Zhou territory, the image of sweeping away filth and establishing the new.>
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu made Luoyang his capital, residing in the lands of Zhou, symbolizing the sweeping away of corruption and the inauguration of a new era.>

sentences.zh: 光武遂將賔客還舂陵。

translations.literal: Guangwu thereupon led his retainers back to Chunling.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu then led his retainers back to Chunling.

sentences.zh: 時伯升已會衆起兵。

translations.literal: At this time Bo Sheng had already gathered a multitude and raised troops.
translations.idiomatic: By this time, Bo Sheng had already assembled a crowd and raised an army.

sentences.zh: 初，諸家子弟恐懼，皆亡逃自匿，曰：「伯升殺我」。

translations.literal: Initially, the sons of the various families were fearful, all fled and hid themselves, saying: 'Bo Sheng will kill me.'
translations.idiomatic: At first, the young men from the various families were terrified and all fled to hide themselves, saying: 'Bo Sheng is going to kill me.'

sentences.zh: 及見光武絳衣大冠，

translations.literal: When they saw Guangwu in crimson robes and great crown,
translations.idiomatic: But when they saw Guangwu in crimson robes and a grand hat,

sentences.zh: 〈董巴輿服志曰：「大冠者，謂武冠，武官冠之。

translations.literal: <Dong Ba's Treatise on Ceremonial Attire says: 'Great crown means the martial crown, worn by martial officials.'
translations.idiomatic: <Dong Ba's Treatise on Ceremonial Regalia states: 'The grand hat refers to the martial crown, worn by military officials.'

sentences.zh: 」《東觀記》曰：「上時絳衣大冠，將軍服也。」〉

translations.literal: >The Dongguan Records say: 'At this time the emperor wore crimson robes and great crown, the uniform of a general.'>
translations.idiomatic: >The Dongguan Records note: 'At that time the emperor wore crimson robes and a grand hat, the uniform of a general.'>

sentences.zh: 皆驚曰：「謹厚者亦復為之」，乃稍自安。

translations.literal: All were astonished, saying: 'Even the prudent and honest one is doing it too,' thereupon they gradually calmed themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Everyone was astonished, saying: 'Even the cautious and upright one is joining them,' and gradually they became reassured.

sentences.zh: 伯升於是招新市、平林兵，

translations.literal: Bo Sheng thereupon recruited soldiers from Xinshi and Pinglin,
translations.idiomatic: Bo Sheng then recruited troops from Xinshi and Pinglin,

sentences.zh: 〈新市，縣，屬江夏郡，故城在今郢州富水縣東北。

translations.literal: <Xinshi is a county belonging to Jiangxia commandery, its old city is northeast of Fushui county in Yingzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: <Xinshi was a county under Jiangxia commandery; its ancient site lies northeast of Fushui county in present-day Yingzhou.

sentences.zh: 平林，地名，在今隨州隨縣東北。〉

translations.literal: Pinglin is a place name, located northeast of Sui county in Suizhou today.>
translations.idiomatic: Pinglin was a place name, situated northeast of Sui county in present-day Suizhou.>

sentences.zh: 與其帥王鳳、陳牧西擊長聚。

translations.literal: And with their leaders Wang Feng and Chen Mu attacked Changju to the west.
translations.idiomatic: And together with their commanders Wang Feng and Chen Mu attacked the settlement of Changju to the west.

sentences.zh: 〈《廣雅》曰：「聚，居也，音慈諭反。

translations.literal: <The Guangya says: 'Ju means dwelling, pronounced ci yu fan.'
translations.idiomatic: <The Guangya dictionary states: 'Ju means residence, pronounced ci yu fan.'

sentences.zh: 」《前書》音義曰：「小於郷曰聚。」〉

translations.literal: >The Former Book's Phonetic Explanations say: 'A ju is smaller than a xiang.'>
translations.idiomatic: >The Former Han's phonetic glosses state: 'A ju is smaller than a xiang.'>

sentences.zh: 光武初騎牛，殺新野尉乃得馬。

translations.literal: At first Guangwu rode an ox; only after killing the Xinyye magistrate did he obtain a horse.
translations.idiomatic: Initially Guangwu rode an ox; he only acquired a horse after killing the magistrate of Xinyye.

sentences.zh: 〈《前書》曰，尉，秦官，秩四百石至二百石也。〉

translations.literal: <The Former Book says: Wei is a Qin office, with salary rank from 400 to 200 shi.>
translations.idiomatic: <According to the History of the Former Han: Wei was an office established in the Qin dynasty, with salaries ranging from 400 to 200 shi.>

translations.idiomatic: Advanced and slaughtered the inhabitants of Tangzi village,

translations.idiomatic: <Convention states: 'Massacring means executing many.'

translations.idiomatic: >Tangzi village includes Tangzi mountain, located southwest of Huyang county in present-day Tangzhou.>

translations.literal: Also killed the Huyang magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: Also executed the magistrate of Huyang.

translations.literal: <Huyang belonged to Nanyang commandery, now Tangzhou county.
translations.idiomatic: <Huyang was under Nanyang commandery, now equivalent to Tangzhou county.

sentences.zh: 《東觀記》曰：「劉終詐稱江夏吏，誘殺之。」〉

translations.literal: The Dongguan Records say: 'Liu Zhong falsely claimed to be a Jiangxia official and lured him to be killed.'>
translations.idiomatic: According to the Dongguan Records: 'Liu Zhong falsely pretended to be a Jiangxia official and tricked him into being killed.'>

sentences.zh: 軍中分財物不均，衆恚恨，欲反攻諸劉。

translations.literal: In the army the division of goods was unequal, the multitude was resentful and angry, wanting to turn back and attack the various Liu clans.
translations.idiomatic: Within the army, the distribution of spoils was unfair, causing widespread resentment and anger, with troops wanting to turn against the various Liu clans.

sentences.zh: 光武斂宗人所得物，悉以與之，衆乃恱。

translations.literal: Guangwu gathered the goods obtained by his clansmen and gave all of them to the troops, the multitude then became pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu collected all the goods his clansmen had acquired and distributed them entirely to the soldiers, whereupon the troops became content.

sentences.zh: 進拔棘陽，

translations.literal: Advanced and captured Jiyang,
translations.idiomatic: Advanced and seized Jiyang,

translations.literal: <County name, belonging to Nanyang commandery, on the north bank of Jishui, ancient state of Xie, old city northwest of Huyang county in Tangzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: <County name under Nanyang commandery, located north of the Jishui River, site of the ancient state of Xie; its former city lies northwest of Huyang county in present-day Tangzhou.

translations.idiomatic: And battled Wang Mang's Front Division Grand Master Zhen Fu,

translations.literal: <Wang Mang established six divisions, each commandery had one grand master, duties like a governor.
translations.idiomatic: <Wang Mang created six military divisions, with each commandery appointing one grand master whose responsibilities were equivalent to a governor.

sentences.zh: 南陽為前隊，河內為後隊，潁川為左隊，弘農為右隊，河東為兆隊，滎陽為祈隊。

translations.idiomatic: Nanyang formed the front division, Henei the rear division, Yingchuan the left division, Hongnong the right division, Hedong the vanguard division, and Xingyang the prayer division.

sentences.zh: 隊音遂。〉

translations.idiomatic: Dui is pronounced sui.>

translations.idiomatic: And Assistant Commander Liangqiu Ci

translations.literal: <Wang Mang placed one assistant commander in each division, duties like a commandery captain.>
translations.idiomatic: <Wang Mang appointed one assistant commander to each division, with responsibilities equivalent to a commandery captain.>

sentences.zh: 戰於小長安，

translations.literal: Fought at Xiaochangan,
translations.idiomatic: Battled at Xiaochangan,

translations.idiomatic: <According to the Xu Han Shu, Yuanyang county contains the Xiaochangan settlement; its ancient site lies south of Nanyang county in present-day Dengzhou.>

sentences.zh: 漢軍大敗，還保棘陽。

translations.literal: The Han army suffered a great defeat and retreated to defend Jiyang.
translations.idiomatic: The Han forces suffered a major defeat and withdrew to defend Jiyang.

sentences.zh: 二月辛巳，立劉聖公為天子，以伯升為大司徒，光武為太常偏將軍。

translations.literal: In the second month on xinsi day, Liu Shenggong was established as Son of Heaven, with Bo Sheng as Grand Minister of Works and Guangwu as Assistant General of the Grand Minister of Ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: On the xinsi day of the second month, Liu Shenggong was enthroned as Son of Heaven, with Bo Sheng appointed Grand Minister of Works and Guangwu as Assistant General under the Grand Minister of Ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 〈《前書》曰：「奉常，秦官。

translations.literal: <The Former Book says: 'Fengchang is a Qin office.'
translations.idiomatic: <According to the History of the Former Han: 'Fengchang was an office established in the Qin dynasty.'

sentences.zh: 景帝更名太常。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing changed the name to Taichang.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing renamed it Taichang.

sentences.zh: 」應劭漢官儀曰：「欲令國家盛大，社稷常存，故稱太常。

translations.literal: >Ying Shao's Han Offices says: 'To make the state flourish greatly and the altars of soil and grain endure eternally, therefore called Taichang.'
translations.idiomatic: >Ying Shao's Han Official System states: 'To ensure the nation flourishes greatly and the altars of soil and grain endure forever, therefore called Taichang.'

sentences.zh: 」老子曰：「偏將軍處左，上將軍處右。

translations.literal: >Laozi says: 'The assistant general is positioned on the left, the supreme general on the right.'
translations.idiomatic: >Laozi states: 'The assistant general stands on the left, the supreme general on the right.'

sentences.zh: 」《東觀記》曰：「時無印，得定武侯家丞印，佩之入朝。」〉

sentences.zh: 初，王莽徵天下能為兵法者六十三家數百人，並以為軍吏；

translations.literal: Initially, Wang Mang summoned from throughout the empire sixty-three families skilled in military tactics, several hundred persons, and all appointed them as army officers;
translations.idiomatic: At first, Wang Mang summoned sixty-three families skilled in military strategy from across the empire, several hundred individuals in total, and appointed them all as army officers;

sentences.zh: 選練武衞，招募猛士，

translations.literal: Selected and trained martial guards, recruited fierce warriors,
translations.idiomatic: Selected and drilled martial guards, recruited valiant fighters,

translations.literal: Banners and baggage trains stretched for a thousand li without end.
translations.idiomatic: Banners and supply wagons extended for a thousand li without interruption.

sentences.zh: 〈《周禮》曰：「析羽為旌，熊虎為旗。

translations.literal: <The Zhou Rites says: 'Feathers split make jing, bears and tigers make qi.'
translations.idiomatic: <According to the Rites of Zhou: 'Split feathers make banners, bears and tigers make flags.'

translations.idiomatic: >Zi refers to a type of cart.

translations.idiomatic: The Explanation of Names states: 'Zi means mixed together.'

translations.literal: Meaning military grain and various items are loaded mixed together.
translations.idiomatic: Meaning that military rations and various supplies are loaded together in a jumbled manner.

sentences.zh: 以其累重，故稱輜重。

translations.literal: Because they are cumbersome, therefore called zi zhong.
translations.idiomatic: Because they are heavy and burdensome, therefore called baggage.

sentences.zh: 」重音直用反。〉

translations.idiomatic: >Zhong is pronounced zhi yong fan.>

sentences.zh: 時有長人巨無霸，

translations.literal: At this time there was a tall man Ju Wuba,
translations.idiomatic: At this time there was a giant named Ju Wuba,

sentences.zh: 〈王莽連率韓博上言：「有竒士，長一丈，大十圍，自謂巨無霸，出於蓬萊東南五城西北，詔如海濵，軺車不能載，三馬不能勝，卧則枕鼓，以鐵箸食。

translations.literal: <Wang Mang's Protector-General Han Bo submitted a memorial: 'There is a strange warrior, ten chi tall, ten wei in girth, who calls himself Ju Wuba, emerging from northwest of the Five Cities southeast of Penglai, his feet like the seashore, light carriages cannot carry him, three horses cannot pull him, when sleeping he pillows drums, eats with iron chopsticks.'
translations.idiomatic: <Wang Mang's Regional Commander Han Bo submitted a memorial stating: 'There is a remarkable warrior, ten chi tall and ten wei in circumference, who calls himself Ju Wuba. He emerged from northwest of the Five Cities southeast of Penglai. His feet are like the seashore. Light carriages cannot carry him; three horses cannot pull him. When sleeping he uses drums as pillows and eats with iron chopsticks.'

translations.literal: >See the Former Book.>
translations.idiomatic: >See the History of the Former Han.>

sentences.zh: 長一丈，大十圍，以為壘尉；

translations.literal: Ten chi tall, ten wei in girth, appointed as Fortification Captain;
translations.idiomatic: Ten chi tall and ten wei in circumference, appointed as Fortification Officer;

sentences.zh: 〈鄭玄注周禮云：「軍壁曰壘。

translations.literal: <Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Zhou Rites says: 'Military ramparts are called lei.'
translations.idiomatic: <Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Zhou Rites states: 'Military fortifications are called ramparts.'

sentences.zh: 」崔瑗中壘校尉箴曰：「堂堂黃帝，設為壘壁。

translations.literal: >Cui Yuan's Admonition for the Central Fortification Colonel says: 'Majestic Yellow Emperor established ramparts and walls.'
translations.idiomatic: >Cui Yuan's Admonition for the Central Rampart Colonel states: 'The majestic Yellow Emperor established fortifications and walls.'

sentences.zh: 」尉者主壘壁之事。〉

translations.literal: >Wei officers oversee rampart matters.>
translations.idiomatic: >Captains are responsible for matters concerning fortifications.>

sentences.zh: 又驅諸猛獸

translations.literal: Also drove various fierce beasts
translations.idiomatic: Also drove various ferocious beasts

translations.idiomatic: <'Meng' is alternatively written as 'guang'.

translations.literal: Guang, fierce appearance, pronounced gu meng fan.>
translations.idiomatic: Guang means fierce appearance, pronounced gu meng fan.>

sentences.zh: 虎豹犀象之屬，以助威武。

translations.literal: Tigers, leopards, rhinoceroses, elephants and such, to assist in martial prowess.
translations.idiomatic: Tigers, leopards, rhinoceroses, elephants and similar beasts, to enhance martial prestige.

sentences.zh: 自秦、漢出師之盛，未甞有也。

translations.literal: Since the Qin and Han military campaigns at their height, there has never been such.
translations.idiomatic: Since the peak military campaigns of the Qin and Han dynasties, there has never been anything like this.

sentences.zh: 光武將數千兵，徼之於陽關。

translations.literal: Guangwu led several thousand troops and intercepted them at Yangguan.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu led several thousand soldiers and ambushed them at Yangguan.

sentences.zh: 〈聚名也。

translations.literal: <Ju name also.
translations.idiomatic: <Also a name for a settlement.

translations.idiomatic: Li Yuan's Commentary on the Classic of Waterways states: 'The Ying River flows southeast past the Yangguan settlement, with settlements facing each other across the Ying River.'

translations.literal: >Located northwest of Yangdi county in Luozhou today.>
translations.idiomatic: >Located northwest of Yangdi county in present-day Luozhou.>

sentences.zh: 諸將見尋、邑兵盛，反走，馳入昆陽，皆惶怖，憂念妻孥，

translations.literal: The various generals saw that Xun and Yi's troops were numerous, turned back and fled, galloped into Kunyang, all terrified, worried about their wives and children,
translations.idiomatic: When the various generals saw that Xun and Yi's forces were so numerous, they turned back in flight, galloped into Kunyang, all filled with terror, anxious about their wives and children,

sentences.zh: 〈孥，子也。〉

translations.literal: <Nu means children.>
translations.idiomatic: <Nu means offspring.>

sentences.zh: 欲散歸諸城。

translations.literal: Wanting to disperse and return to various cities.
translations.idiomatic: Wanting to scatter and return to their various cities.

sentences.zh: 光武議曰：「今兵糓旣少，而外寇彊大，并力禦之，功庶可立；

translations.literal: Guangwu said: 'Now our troops and grain are already few, but the external bandits are strong and numerous, if we combine forces to resist them, merit can perhaps be established;
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu addressed them: 'Our forces and provisions are already meager, but the enemy outside is powerful and numerous. If we unite our strength to resist them, we may yet achieve merit;

sentences.zh: 如欲分散，勢無俱全。

translations.literal: If we want to disperse, the situation will not allow all to survive intact.'
translations.idiomatic: If we disperse, none of us will survive unscathed.'

sentences.zh: 且宛城未拔，

translations.literal: Moreover, Wancheng has not been captured,
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, Wancheng has not yet fallen,

sentences.zh: 〈謂伯升圍之未拔也。〉

translations.literal: <Meaning Bo Sheng's siege of it has not succeeded.>
translations.idiomatic: <Meaning Bo Sheng's siege has not yet taken it.>

sentences.zh: 不能相救，昆陽即破，一日之閒，諸部亦滅矣。

translations.literal: Unable to rescue each other, Kunyang will immediately be broken, within a day's time, all the various divisions will also be destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: Unable to aid one another, Kunyang will fall immediately, and within a single day, all our various units will be annihilated as well.

sentences.zh: 今不同心膽共舉功名，反欲守妻子財物邪？

translations.literal: Now if we do not unite our hearts and courage to achieve merit and fame together, do you instead wish to protect your wives, children, and property?
translations.idiomatic: If we do not now unite our hearts and resolve to achieve merit and fame together, do you wish instead to protect your wives, children, and possessions?

sentences.zh: 」諸將怒曰：「劉將軍何敢如是！

translations.literal: >The various generals angrily said: 'General Liu, how dare you speak like this!'
translations.idiomatic: >The various generals angrily exclaimed: 'General Liu, how dare you speak in such a manner!'

sentences.zh: 」光武笑而起。

translations.literal: >Guangwu smiled and stood up.
translations.idiomatic: >Guangwu smiled and rose to his feet.

sentences.zh: 會候騎還，言大兵且至城北，軍陳數百里，不見其後。

translations.literal: Just then the scouting cavalry returned, saying that a large army was about to arrive north of the city, with military formations stretching several hundred li, no end visible.
translations.idiomatic: Just then the scouting cavalry returned, reporting that a massive army was approaching north of the city, with battle formations stretching hundreds of li, their rear invisible.

sentences.zh: 諸將遽相謂曰：「更請劉將軍計之。

translations.literal: The various generals hurriedly said to each other: 'Let us ask General Liu for his plan again.'
translations.idiomatic: The various generals hurriedly said to one another: 'Let us consult General Liu's strategy once more.'

sentences.zh: 」光武復為圖畫成敗。

translations.literal: >Guangwu again laid out the prospects for success and failure.
translations.idiomatic: >Guangwu once more outlined the possibilities of victory and defeat.

sentences.zh: 諸將憂迫，皆曰「諾」。

translations.literal: The various generals, anxious and pressed, all said 'Yes.'
translations.idiomatic: The various generals, filled with worry and urgency, all agreed.

sentences.zh: 時城中唯有八九千人，光武乃使成國上公王鳳、廷尉大將軍王常留守，夜自與驃騎大將軍宗佻、

translations.literal: At this time there were only eight or nine thousand men in the city. Guangwu then had the Duke of Chengguo Wang Feng and Grand General of the Court of Justice Wang Chang remain to defend it, while he himself at night went with the Grand General of Chariot Cavalry Zong Tiao,
translations.idiomatic: At this time the city held only eight or nine thousand men. Guangwu ordered the Duke of Chengguo Wang Feng and Grand General of the Court of Judicial Review Wang Chang to remain and defend it, while he himself went out at night with the Grand General of Agile Cavalry Zong Tiao,

sentences.zh: 〈驃騎大將軍，武帝置，自霍去病始。

translations.literal: <Grand General of Chariot Cavalry was established by Emperor Wu, beginning with Huo Qubing.
translations.idiomatic: <The Grand General of Agile Cavalry was created by Emperor Wu, first held by Huo Qubing.

sentences.zh: 佻音太堯反。〉

translations.literal: Tiao pronounced tai yao fan.>
translations.idiomatic: Tiao is pronounced tai yao fan.>

sentences.zh: 五威將軍李軼等十三騎，

translations.literal: Five Majestic General Li Yi and thirteen other horsemen,
translations.idiomatic: Five Majestic General Li Yi and thirteen other cavalrymen,

sentences.zh: 〈王莽置五威將軍，其衣服依五方之色，以威天下。

translations.literal: <Wang Mang established the Five Majestic Generals, whose clothing followed the colors of the five directions, to awe the realm.
translations.idiomatic: <Wang Mang created the Five Majestic Generals, whose robes followed the colors of the five cardinal directions to intimidate the empire.

sentences.zh: 李軼初起，猶假以為號。〉

translations.literal: Li Yi at first used it as a borrowed title.>
translations.idiomatic: Li Yi initially adopted it as a provisional title.>

sentences.zh: 出城南門，於外收兵。

translations.literal: Went out the south gate of the city and gathered troops outside.
translations.idiomatic: Exited through the south gate of the city and assembled troops beyond the walls.

sentences.zh: 時莽軍到城下者且十萬，光武幾不得出。

translations.literal: At this time Wang Mang's army that had arrived at the city walls numbered nearly 100,000, Guangwu almost could not get out.
translations.idiomatic: At this moment, Wang Mang's forces that had reached the city walls numbered nearly 100,000; Guangwu almost could not break out.

sentences.zh: 〈幾音祈。〉

translations.literal: <Ji pronounced qi.>
translations.idiomatic: <Ji is pronounced qi.>

sentences.zh: 旣至郾、定陵，悉發諸營兵，而諸將貪惜財貨，欲分留守之。

translations.literal: Having reached Yan and Dingling, they mobilized all the camp troops, but the various generals were greedy for goods and wanted to divide them up for garrison duty.
translations.idiomatic: Upon reaching Yan and Dingling, they mobilized troops from all the camps, but the various generals, greedy for spoils, wanted to divide them up and leave some behind for defense.

sentences.zh: 光武曰：「今若破敵，珍珤萬倍，

translations.literal: Guangwu said: 'If we defeat the enemy now, precious treasures will be ten thousand times greater,
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu said: 'If we defeat the enemy now, precious treasures will multiply ten thousand times,

translations.literal: <Bao is the ancient character for 'bao'.>
translations.idiomatic: <Bao is the ancient form of the character 'bao' (treasure).>

sentences.zh: 大功可成；

translations.literal: great merit can be achieved;
translations.idiomatic: great merit can be accomplished;

sentences.zh: 如為所敗，首領無餘，何財物之有！

translations.literal: if we are defeated by them, not a head will remain, what goods will there be!'
translations.idiomatic: if we are defeated by them, not a single head will survive—what goods would remain!'

sentences.zh: 」衆乃從。

translations.literal: >The multitude then followed.
translations.idiomatic: >The multitude then complied.

translations.idiomatic: Yan You advised Wang Yi: 'Kunyang city is small but impregnable. The pretender to the throne is currently in Wan. Quickly advance the main army

translations.literal: <Ji means urgent, pronounced ji li fan.>
translations.idiomatic: <Ji means urgent, pronounced ji li fan.>

sentences.zh: ，彼必奔走；

translations.literal: they will certainly flee in panic;
translations.idiomatic: and they will surely flee in disorder;

translations.literal: when Wan is defeated, Kunyang will submit on its own.'
translations.idiomatic: once Wan falls, Kunyang will surrender without a fight.'

translations.literal: >Yi said: 'I once as Tiger Fang General surrounded Zhai Yi, but because I did not capture him alive, I was blamed and reprimanded.'
translations.idiomatic: >Yi replied: 'Once I surrounded Zhai Yi as Tiger Fang General, but because I failed to capture him alive, I was censured and reprimanded.'

sentences.zh: 〈翟義字文仲，方進少子，為東郡太守。

translations.literal: <Zhai Yi, courtesy name Wenzhong, youngest son of Fang Jin, served as Grand Administrator of Dongjun.
translations.idiomatic: <Zhai Yi, courtesy name Wenzhong, was the youngest son of Fang Jin and served as Grand Administrator of Dongjun commandery.

sentences.zh: 王莽居攝，義心惡之，乃立東平王雲子信為天子，義自號柱天大將軍，以誅莽。

translations.literal: Wang Mang served as regent, Yi's heart hated it, so he established Dongping King Yun's son Xin as Son of Heaven, Yi called himself Pillar Heaven Grand General to execute Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang served as regent, Yi hated him in his heart, so he enthroned Xin, son of Dongping King Yun, as Son of Heaven. Yi proclaimed himself Pillar Heaven Grand General to execute Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 莽乃使孫建、王邑等將兵擊義，破之。

translations.literal: Wang Mang then sent Sun Jian, Wang Yi and others to lead troops and attack Yi, defeating him.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang then dispatched Sun Jian, Wang Yi, and others to lead troops against Yi, defeating him.

sentences.zh: 義亡，自殺，故坐不生得。

translations.literal: Yi died; he killed himself, and therefore on account of the offense he was not taken alive.
translations.idiomatic: Yi was dead—he had taken his own life—so under the law he counted as never having been captured alive.

sentences.zh: 坐音才卧反。

translations.literal: For the graph glossed here (the word for 'offense' in this legal phrase) the fanqie is cai-wo.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary records the fanqie reading cai-wo for the word taken as 'offense' in this sentence.

sentences.zh: 見《前書》。〉

translations.literal: See the Former Han History.>
translations.idiomatic: See the Book of Former Han.>

sentences.zh: 今將百萬之衆，遇城而不能下，何謂邪？」

translations.literal: Now I lead a million men, yet I cannot take a city I meet—what does that mean?"
translations.idiomatic: Here I command a host in the hundreds of thousands, yet a single city blocks me—what kind of generalship is that?"

translations.literal: <Some manuscripts read one graph as 'pass by' in place of 'meet'.>
translations.idiomatic: <One variant substitutes the graph meaning 'pass by' for the one meaning 'encounter'.>

sentences.zh: 遂圍之數十重，列營百數，雲車十餘丈，

translations.literal: Thereupon they surrounded it in dozens of rings, set up hundreds of camps, and raised cloud-ladders over ten zhang high,
translations.idiomatic: They ringed the city in layer upon layer, pitched camp after camp—hundreds of them—and rolled up siege towers, the so-called cloud-ladders, more than ten zhang tall,

sentences.zh: 〈雲車即樓車，稱雲，言其高也，升之以望敵，猶墨子云「公輸般為雲梯之械」。〉

translations.literal: <The cloud-ladder is the towered assault cart; it is called 'cloud' because of its height; one mounts it to spy on the enemy, as Mozi says 'Gongshu Ban made the cloud-ladder device.'>
translations.idiomatic: <Commentary: the 'cloud-cart' is the assault tower; the name evokes height. Troops climbed it to overlook the walls—Mozi records Gongshu Ban's 'cloud-ladder' machines.>

sentences.zh: 瞰臨城中，

translations.literal: looking down into the city,
translations.idiomatic: from which they peered straight down into the streets below,

sentences.zh: 〈俯視曰瞰，音苦暫反。〉

translations.literal: <To look down from above is the word here glossed; the fanqie is ku-zan.>
translations.idiomatic: <The gloss defines the verb as 'look down from a height' and gives the fanqie ku-zan.>

sentences.zh: 旗幟蔽野，

translations.literal: banners and streamers hid the plain,
translations.idiomatic: flags and pennants blotted out the open ground,

sentences.zh: 〈《廣雅》曰：「幟，幡也，音熾。」〉

translations.literal: <The Guangya says: 'The word for a long banner is read like the graph for blaze (zhi).'>
translations.idiomatic: <The Guangya defines the streamer word and assigns it the same reading as 'blaze' (zhi).>

sentences.zh: 埃塵連天，鉦鼓之聲聞數百里。

translations.literal: dust rose to join the sky, and the sound of gongs and drums was heard for several hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: a haze of dust climbed to the heavens, while gongs and drums rolled for hundreds of li around.

sentences.zh: 〈《說文》曰：「鉦，鐃也，似鈴。」〉

translations.literal: <The Shuowen says: 'The bronze signal bell is a kind of nao, shaped like a small bell.'>
translations.idiomatic: <The Shuowen classifies the signal bell as a variety of nao, hand-bell sized.>

sentences.zh: 或為地道，衝輣橦城。

translations.literal: Some dug tunnels; battering-rams pounded the walls.
translations.idiomatic: Sappers drove tunnels; rams and crowbars hammered the battlements.

sentences.zh: 〈衝，橦車也。

translations.literal: <The ram is the battering-engine.
translations.idiomatic: <The battering-ram is the assault engine.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「臨衝閑閑。

translations.literal: The Odes say: 'The rams advanced, leisurely, leisurely.'
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry has: 'Their rams rolled up, slow and steady.'

sentences.zh: 」許慎曰：「輣，樓車也。

translations.literal: Xu Shen says: 'The siege tower cart is the mobile assault tower.'
translations.idiomatic: Xu Shen glosses the siege-tower cart as the tall rolling tower.

sentences.zh: 」輣音步耕反。〉

translations.literal: ' The siege-tower graph is read bu-geng fan.>
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives bu-geng as the fanqie for the siege-tower graph.>

sentences.zh: 積弩亂發，矢下如雨，城中負戶而汲。

translations.literal: Repeating crossbows shot wildly; arrows fell like rain; inside the city people drew water with their backs to the door.
translations.idiomatic: Volley-fire from heavy crossbows darkened the air; bolts fell like rain, and defenders had to hug their doors just to reach the wells.

sentences.zh: 王鳳等乞降，不許。

translations.literal: Wang Feng and the others begged to surrender; permission was refused.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng's party offered to yield; Wang Xun and Wang Yi would not hear of it.

sentences.zh: 尋、邑自以為功在漏刻，意氣甚逸。

translations.literal: Xun and Yi thought success was only a matter of moments; their spirits were wildly elated.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xun and Wang Yi were sure the city would fall within the hour; they grew cocky beyond measure.

sentences.zh: 夜有流星墜營中，晝有雲如壞山，當營而隕，不及地尺而散，吏士皆厭伏。

translations.literal: By night a shooting star fell into the camp; by day a cloud like a crumbling mountain hung over the camp and fell, scattering a chi above the ground; officers and men all cowered.
translations.idiomatic: A meteor streaked into their lines at night; by day a mountain-shaped cloud broke over the encampment and dissolved a foot short of the earth—every soldier went cold with dread.

sentences.zh: 〈《續漢志》曰：「雲如壞山，謂營頭之星也。

translations.literal: <The Continued Han Treatise says: 'A cloud like a crumbling mountain is the camp-head star.
translations.idiomatic: <The Xu Han zhi explains the omen: a cloud like a shattered peak is the camp-head phenomenon.

sentences.zh: 占曰：『營頭之所墜，其下覆軍殺將，血流千里。

translations.literal: The divination says: 'Where the camp-head falls, below it armies are overthrown and generals slain; blood flows for a thousand li.'
translations.idiomatic: Prognostication reads: 'Where that star strikes, hosts perish and commanders die; blood may wash a thousand li.'

sentences.zh: 』」厭音一葉反。〉

translations.literal: '" The graph glossed here is read yi-ye (meaning to cower).>
translations.idiomatic: The graph in question is read yi-ye, meaning to shrink or cower.>

sentences.zh: 六月己卯，光武遂與營部俱進，自將步騎千餘，前去大軍四五里而陳。

translations.literal: On the day jimao of the sixth month, Guangwu then advanced together with the camp divisions, personally leading a little over a thousand foot and horse, going forward four or five li ahead of the main host and drawing up formation.
translations.idiomatic: On jimao in the sixth month Guangwu moved out with the allied camps, taking personal command of just over a thousand cavalry and infantry, and halted four or five li in front of the main army to form line.

sentences.zh: 尋、邑亦遣兵數千合戰。

translations.literal: Xun and Yi also sent several thousand troops to join battle.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xun and Wang Yi likewise detached a few thousand men to meet him.

sentences.zh: 光武奔之，斬首數十級。

translations.literal: Guangwu charged them and beheaded several tens of heads.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu drove straight in and took several dozen heads in the first clash.

sentences.zh: 〈秦法，斬首一，賜爵一級，故因謂斬首為級。〉

translations.literal: <Under Qin law each enemy head earned one noble rank, so decapitations came to be counted as 'rank tallies.'>
translations.idiomatic: <Qin practice awarded a noble grade per head; hence military tallies were still spoken of as rank-counts from beheadings.>

sentences.zh: 諸部喜曰：「劉將軍平生見小敵怯，今見大敵勇，甚可怪也，且復居前。

translations.literal: The various divisions rejoiced, saying: 'General Liu in his life has shown fear before small foes; now he shows courage before a great foe—very strange indeed; moreover he again takes the front.
translations.idiomatic: The other columns exclaimed: 'General Liu has always hung back against petty enemies—now he throws himself at the mighty host! How odd—and he is out in front again.'

sentences.zh: 請助將軍！

translations.literal: Please assist the general!"
translations.idiomatic: Let us reinforce him!"

sentences.zh: 」光武復進，尋、邑兵却，諸部共乘之，斬首數百千級。

translations.literal: " Guangwu advanced again; Xun and Yi's troops gave way; the various divisions together pressed them and beheaded several hundred or thousand heads.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu pressed the attack; Wang Xun's line buckled; the allied units piled in and piled up hundreds upon hundreds of heads.

sentences.zh: 連勝，遂前。

translations.literal: Victory followed victory, and they went forward.
translations.idiomatic: Win followed win, and the Han vanguard kept pushing.

sentences.zh: 時伯升拔宛已三日，而光武尚未知，乃偽使持書報城中，云「宛下兵到」，而陽墯其書。

translations.literal: At that time Bo Sheng had taken Wan three days before, but Guangwu still did not know; he therefore feigned to send a messenger with a letter reporting to the city, saying 'The relief army from fallen Wan has arrived,' and deliberately dropped the letter.
translations.idiomatic: Bo Sheng had captured Wan three days earlier, though Guangwu had not yet heard; he had a courier 'lose' a dispatch to the besieged city claiming that Wan's garrison was marching to the rescue.

sentences.zh: 尋、邑得之，不憙。

translations.literal: When Xun and Yi obtained it, they were not pleased.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Xun and Wang Yi read it, their faces fell.

sentences.zh: 〈憙音許記反。〉

translations.literal: <The graph here (pleased) is read xu-ji.>
translations.idiomatic: <The graph meaning 'pleased' is glossed xu-ji.>

sentences.zh: 諸將旣經累捷，膽氣益壯，無不一當百。

translations.literal: The generals, having passed through repeated victories, grew bolder; there was not one who did not match a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Flush with a run of successes, every officer fought as if he were worth a hundred men.

sentences.zh: 光武乃與敢死者三千人，從城西水上衝其中堅，

translations.literal: Guangwu then, with three thousand dare-to-die men, from the water west of the city charged their central elite,
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu led three thousand volunteers along the stream west of Kunyang and rammed the enemy's iron core,

sentences.zh: 〈敢死謂果敢而死者。

translations.literal: <Dare-to-die means those who are resolute unto death.
translations.idiomatic: <'Dare-to-die' troops were picked men sworn to fight to the last.

sentences.zh: 凡軍事，中軍將最尊，居中以堅銳自輔，故曰中堅也。〉

translations.literal: In general military usage the commander-in-chief is most honored; stationed in the center he supports himself with the hardest troops, therefore it is called the 'central firm (中堅).'>
translations.idiomatic: In Han usage the general-in-chief stood in the middle, screened by his best unit—hence the phrase 'central firm' for that crack corps.>

sentences.zh: 尋、邑陳亂，乘銳崩之，遂殺王尋。

translations.literal: Xun and Yi's formation was thrown into confusion; riding their keen edge they shattered it, and thereupon killed Wang Xun.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xun's line collapsed; Guangwu's men rode the momentum and cut down Wang Xun himself.

sentences.zh: 城中亦鼓譟而出，中外合埶，震呼動天地，莽兵大潰，走者相騰踐，奔殪百餘里閒。

translations.literal: Those inside the city also beat drums and sallied forth; inner and outer combined force; shouts shook heaven and earth; Mang's troops broke utterly; those who fled trampled one another; corpses littered the ground for more than a hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: The defenders thundered out to join the attack; inner blow and outer blow met; Mang's host disintegrated into a stampede that left the plain strewn with bodies for a hundred li.

sentences.zh: 〈殪，仆也，音於計反。

translations.literal: <The word glossed means to fall flat; the fanqie is yu-ji.
translations.idiomatic: <The word means to fall prostrate; the reading given is yu-ji.

sentences.zh: 或作「噎」。〉

translations.literal: Some texts write the homophone meaning 'choke' instead.>
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts use the homophone meaning 'to choke' instead.>

sentences.zh: 會大雷風，屋瓦皆飛，雨下如注，滍川盛溢，

translations.literal: There came great thunder and wind; roof tiles flew; rain poured like poured water; the Zhi River swelled and overflowed,
translations.idiomatic: A storm broke—tiles flew, rain fell in sheets, and the Zhi torrent rose until its banks burst,

sentences.zh: 〈水經曰，滍水出南陽魯陽縣西堯山，東南經昆陽城北，東入汝。

translations.literal: <The Shuijing says: the Zhi River rises west of Mount Yao in Luyang county in Nanyang, flows southeast past the north of Kunyang city, and east enters the Ru.
translations.idiomatic: <The Shuijing notes that the Zhi rises west of Mount Yao in Luyang (Nanyang), skirts Kunyang on the north, and joins the Ru to the east.

sentences.zh: 滍音直理反。〉

translations.literal: The river name graph is read zhi-li.>
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives zhi-li as the reading for the river-name graph.>

sentences.zh: 虎豹皆股戰，士卒爭赴，溺死者以萬數，水為不流。

translations.literal: Tigers and leopards all trembled in the thighs; soldiers scrambled to escape; those drowned were reckoned in the tens of thousands; the water ceased to flow.
translations.idiomatic: Even the army's tigers and leopards shook in their haunches; men threw themselves into the flood until corpses choked the current and the river seemed to stand still.

sentences.zh: 〈數過於萬，故以萬為數。〉

translations.literal: <When the number passes ten thousand, therefore ten thousand is used as the unit of reckoning.>
translations.idiomatic: <The note explains that 'by the ten thousands' means a figure exceeding a full wan.>

sentences.zh: 王邑、嚴尤、陳茂輕騎乘死人度水逃去。

translations.literal: Wang Yi, Yan You, and Chen Mao on light horses, riding dead men, crossed the water and fled away.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yi, Yan You, and Chen Mao escaped on fast horses, using floating corpses as rafts to ford the stream.

sentences.zh: 盡獲其軍實輜重，車甲珍寶，不可勝筭，舉之連月不盡，或燔燒其餘。

translations.literal: They captured all their military stores, baggage, chariots, armor, and precious goods beyond calculation; lifting them took months without finishing; some burned the remainder.
translations.idiomatic: The victors seized ordnance, wagons, mail, and treasure beyond counting—months of hauling could not move it all, and much was simply burned where it lay.

sentences.zh: 光武因復徇下潁陽。

translations.literal: Guangwu thereupon again subdued Yingyang.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu then swept on and brought Yingyang to heel.

sentences.zh: 〈縣名，屬潁川郡，故城在今許州。〉

translations.literal: <A county in Yingchuan commandery; its old walled town stood in the Xu prefecture belt of present-day Henan.>
translations.idiomatic: <Yingyang county in Yingchuan; the ancient site lay in what was later called Xu prefecture, in modern Henan.>

sentences.zh: 會伯升為更始所害，光武自父城馳詣宛謝。

translations.literal: It happened that Bo Sheng was killed by the Gengshi Emperor; Guangwu galloped from Fucheng to Wan to offer apologies.
translations.idiomatic: When the Gengshi regime murdered Bo Sheng, Guangwu raced from Fucheng to the capital at Wan to make his submission.

sentences.zh: 〈父城，縣，古應國也，屬潁川郡，故城在今許州葉縣東北。

translations.literal: <Fucheng, a county, the ancient state of Ying, belonging to Yingchuan commandery; its old city is northeast of Ye county in Xuzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: <Fucheng in Yingchuan was the old Ying domain; its ruins lie northeast of Ye county (Xuchang).

sentences.zh: 以伯升見害，心不自安，故謝。〉

translations.literal: Because Bo Sheng had been killed, his heart was not at ease, therefore he apologized.>
translations.idiomatic: He went to disclaim any complicity in his brother's death, for he knew his own position was precarious.>

sentences.zh: 司徒官屬迎弔光武，光武難交私語，深引過而已。

translations.literal: The staff of the Minister of Education came to welcome and condole with Guangwu; Guangwu found it hard to converse privately in friendship; he deeply cited his own faults, that was all.
translations.idiomatic: Minister of Education Liu's subordinates came to offer sympathy; Guangwu refused intimate talk and confined himself to abject self-blame.

sentences.zh: 未甞自伐昆陽之功，又不敢為伯升服喪，飲食言笑如平常。

translations.literal: He never boasted of the Kunyang achievement, nor dared to mourn Bo Sheng; drinking, eating, speech, and laughter were as usual.
translations.idiomatic: He never mentioned the victory at Kunyang, dared not wear mourning for his brother, and kept to ordinary meals and conversation.

sentences.zh: 更始以是慙，拜光武為破虜大將軍，封武信侯。

translations.literal: The Gengshi Emperor therefore was ashamed, and appointed Guangwu General Who Destroys Captives, enfeoffing him as Marquis of Wuxin.
translations.idiomatic: Shamed by such restraint, Emperor Gengshi named him General Who Destroys Captives and Marquis of Wuxin.

sentences.zh: 九月庚戌，三輔豪桀共誅王莽，傳首詣宛。

translations.literal: On the day gengxu of the ninth month, the heroes of the Three Adjuncts together executed Wang Mang and sent his head to Wan.
translations.idiomatic: On gengxu in the ninth month the gentry militias of the capital region slew Wang Mang and forwarded his head to Wan.

sentences.zh: 〈三輔謂京兆、左馮翊、右扶風，共在長安中，分領諸縣。

translations.literal: <The Three Adjuncts means Jingzhao, Zuo Fengyi, and You Fufeng, together in Chang'an, each administering its counties.
translations.idiomatic: <'Three Adjuncts' denotes the metropolitan region—Jingzhao, Left Fengyi, and Right Fufeng—around Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰：「智過百人謂之豪。

translations.literal: The Huainanzi says: 'One whose wisdom surpasses a hundred men is called hao.'
translations.idiomatic: The Huainanzi defines a hao as a man wiser than a hundred ordinary fellows.

sentences.zh: 」白虎通云：「賢萬人曰傑。

translations.literal: The Baihu tong says: 'One worthy among ten thousand men is called jie.'
translations.idiomatic: The Baihu tong calls an outstanding champion a jie—a hero among ten thousand.

sentences.zh: 」時城中少年子弟張魚等攻莽於漸臺，商人杜吳殺莽，校尉公賔就斬莽首，將軍申屠建等傳莽首詣宛。〉

translations.literal: At that time the youths Zhang Yu and others in the city attacked Mang at the Jian Terrace; the merchant Du Wu killed Mang; Colonel Gongbin Jiu cut off Mang's head; Generals Shentu Jian and others transmitted Mang's head to Wan.>
translations.idiomatic: Inside Chang'an, young bravos led by Zhang Yu stormed the Jian Terrace; the shopkeeper Du Wu struck Mang down; Colonel Gongbin Jiu severed the head; generals such as Shentu Jian carried it south to Wan.>

sentences.zh: 更始將北都洛陽，以光武行司隷校尉，使前整修宮府。

translations.literal: The Gengshi Emperor was about to move the capital north to Luoyang; he made Guangwu acting Metropolitan Governor and sent him ahead to repair the palaces and offices.
translations.idiomatic: As Gengshi prepared to shift the court to Luoyang, he put Guangwu in charge as acting Metropolitan Governor to put the western palaces in order.

sentences.zh: 〈《前書》曰：司隷校尉本周官，武帝初置，持節，從中都官徒千二百人，督大姦猾。

translations.literal: <The Former Han History says: the Metropolitan Governor followed Zhou offices; Wudi first established it, bearing credentials, with twelve hundred convict attendants from the capital offices, supervising great wrongdoers.
translations.idiomatic: <The Han shu explains that the Metropolitan Governorship revived a Zhou antecedent; Emperor Wu first instituted it with imperial staff and twelve hundred guards to hunt major criminals.

sentences.zh: 後罷其兵，察三輔、三河、弘農。

translations.literal: Later its troops were abolished; it inspected the Three Adjuncts, Three He, and Hongnong.
translations.idiomatic: Later the armed escort was stripped away, leaving the office to audit the capital districts, the three He commanderies, and Hongnong.

sentences.zh: 秩比二千石。

translations.literal: Its salary grade was equivalent to two thousand shi.
translations.idiomatic: Its rank matched the two-thousand-shi ministers.

sentences.zh: 音義云：「以掌徒隷而巡察，故曰司隷。」〉

translations.literal: One gloss runs: 'Because it supervised convict gangs and conducted inspections, it was called the Metropolitan Governor (convict-inspector).'
translations.idiomatic: One gloss explains that watching convict labor gave the office its name as metropolitan convict-inspector.

sentences.zh: 於是置僚屬，作文移，

translations.literal: Thereupon he established subordinates and drafted official communications,
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu at once set up a full secretariat and issued the standard forms of dispatch,

sentences.zh: 〈《東觀記》曰「文書移與屬縣」也。〉

translations.literal: <The Eastern Lodge Record says 'documents were shifted to the subordinate counties.'>
translations.idiomatic: <The Dongguan ji recalls 'written orders forwarded to every county under his eye.'>

sentences.zh: 從事司察，一如舊章。

translations.literal: the attendants conducting inspections, everything according to the old regulations.
translations.idiomatic: and his investigators policed the region exactly as in Former Han times.

sentences.zh: 〈《續漢書》曰：「司隷置從事史十二人，秩皆百石，主督促文書，察舉非法。」〉

translations.literal: <The Continued Han History says: 'The Metropolitan Governor set twelve attendant clerks at one hundred shi each, charged with pushing paperwork and exposing illegalities.'>
translations.idiomatic: <The Xu Han shu lists twelve attendant investigators at one hundred shi, auditing documents and denouncing abuses.>

sentences.zh: 時三輔吏士東迎更始，見諸將過，皆冠幘，

translations.literal: At that time officers and gentlemen of the Three Adjuncts came east to welcome the Gengshi Emperor; seeing the various generals pass, they all wore head-wraps,
translations.idiomatic: When the local elite rode east to greet Gengshi, they saw imperial generals ride by in nothing but kerchiefs,

sentences.zh: 〈漢官儀曰：「幘者，古之卑賤不冠者之所服也。

translations.literal: <Han guan yi says: 'The ze is what men of low status who do not wear caps used to wear.'
translations.idiomatic: <The Han guan yi remarks that the ze was humble headgear for men who went bareheaded by custom.

sentences.zh: 」方言曰：「覆髻謂之幘，或謂之承露。」〉

translations.literal: The Fangyan says: 'What covers the chignon is called a ze, or sometimes called dew-catcher.'>
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong's Fangyan adds that a wrap over the topknot was also called a 'dew-catcher.'>

sentences.zh: 而服婦人衣，諸于繡镼，

translations.literal: yet they wore women's clothing, embroidered short jackets,
translations.idiomatic: yet those same officers flounced past in women's blouses with embroidered half-sleeves,

sentences.zh: 〈《前書》音義曰：「諸于，大掖衣也，如婦人之褂衣。

translations.literal: <The Former Han yin yi says: 'The zhu-yu is a large-sleeved robe like a woman's jacket.'
translations.idiomatic: <Han shu yin yi describes the zhu-yu as a broad-cut robe resembling a woman's gown.

sentences.zh: 」字書無「镼」字，續漢書作「䘿」，並音其物反。

translations.literal: ' Lexicons omit the rare sleeve graph; the Xu Han shu writes a variant form, both read qi-wu.
translations.idiomatic: Lexicons omit the rare sleeve graph; the Xu Han shu spells it with a variant, both read qi-wu.

sentences.zh: 楊雄方言曰：「襜褕，其短者，自關之西謂之『䘪䘿』。

translations.literal: Yang Xiong's Fangyan says: 'The short chan-yu west of the Pass they call xuan-jue.'
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong records that west of Hangu the short chan-yu robe was called xuan-jue.

sentences.zh: 」郭璞注云：「俗名䘿掖。

translations.literal: Guo Pu's commentary says: 'The common name is jue sleeves.'
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu notes the folk term 'jue-sleeve' for the cut-off cuff.

sentences.zh: 」據此，即是諸于上加繡䘿，如今之半臂也。

translations.literal: According to this, then, it is the zhu-yu with embroidered jue on top, like today's half-arm.
translations.idiomatic: Taken together, the costume was a woman's upper gown with embroidered half-sleeves—much like a modern short jacket.

sentences.zh: 或「繡」下有「擁」字。〉

translations.literal: Perhaps the text should carry the word 'gather' after 'embroidered'.>
translations.idiomatic: Some editions insert 'gather' after 'embroidered' in the compound.>

sentences.zh: 莫不笑之，或有畏而走者。

translations.literal: None did not laugh; some were frightened and ran.
translations.idiomatic: The onlookers roared with laughter—or bolted in superstitious fear.

sentences.zh: 〈《續漢志》曰：「時知者見之，以為服之不中，身之災也，乃奔入邊郡避之。

translations.literal: <The Continued Han Treatise says: 'Those with sense saw that clothing out of rule meant personal disaster; they fled to the border commanderies to escape.'
translations.idiomatic: <The omen treatise remarks that sensible men took the mismatched dress for a portent of doom and fled to the frontier.

sentences.zh: 是服妖也。

translations.literal: This was clothing demonry.
translations.idiomatic: Such dress counted as a 'clothing anomaly.'

sentences.zh: 其後更始遂為赤眉所殺。」〉

translations.literal: Afterwards the Gengshi Emperor was indeed killed by the Red Eyebrows.'>
translations.idiomatic: Sure enough, Gengshi later fell to the Red Eyebrows.>

sentences.zh: 及見司隷僚屬，皆歡喜不自勝。

translations.literal: When they saw the Metropolitan Governor's subordinates, they were joyful beyond bearing.
translations.idiomatic: When the same crowds saw Guangwu's metropolitan staff, joy overwhelmed them.

sentences.zh: 老吏或垂涕曰：「不圖今日復見漢官威儀！

translations.literal: Old clerks some shed tears, saying: 'We did not expect today again to see the majesty of Han officials!'
translations.idiomatic: Veteran clerks wept: 'We never thought we would live to see Han dignity on the roads again!'

sentences.zh: 」由是識者皆屬心焉。

translations.literal: " From this those who understood attached their hearts to him.
translations.idiomatic: Men of judgment knew then where true authority lay.

sentences.zh: 及更始至洛陽，乃遣光武以破虜將軍行大司馬事。

translations.literal: When the Gengshi Emperor reached Luoyang, he then dispatched Guangwu, as General Who Destroys Captives, to act as Grand Marshal.
translations.idiomatic: Once Gengshi settled at Luoyang, he sent Guangwu north as acting Grand Marshal while retaining the title General Who Destroys Captives.

sentences.zh: 十月，持節北度河，

translations.literal: In the tenth month, bearing credentials he crossed the Yellow River northward,
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth month he took imperial insignia and crossed the river into Hebei,

sentences.zh: 〈漢官儀曰：「太尉，秦官也，武帝更名大司馬。

translations.literal: <Han guan yi says: 'The Grand Commandant was a Qin office; Wudi changed the name to Grand Marshal.'
translations.idiomatic: <The Grand Marshal's post was the old Qin Grand Commandant, renamed under Emperor Wu.>

sentences.zh: 」節，所以為信也，以竹為之，柄長八尺，以旄牛尾為其眊三重。

translations.literal: ' The credential is what makes trust; it is made of bamboo, the handle eight chi long, with yak-tail hairs triple-layered for its tuft.
translations.idiomatic: The fu credential was an eight-chi bamboo baton hung with triple strands of yak tail to seal orders.

sentences.zh: 馮衍與田邑書曰：「今以一節之任，建三軍之威，豈特寵其八尺之竹，犛牛之尾哉！

translations.literal: Feng Yan wrote to Tian Yi: 'Now with the charge of one credential you establish the awe of three armies—is it only favor for an eight-chi stick and yak tail?'
translations.idiomatic: Feng Yan once asked Tian Yi whether such awe rested on 'nothing but eight chi of bamboo and a yak's tail.'

sentences.zh: 」《續漢志》曰：「更始時，南方有童謠云：『諧不諧，在赤眉；

translations.literal: ' The Continued Han Treatise says: 'During Gengshi there was a children's rhyme in the south: 'Harmony or not lies with the Red Eyebrows;
translations.idiomatic: The Xu Han zhi preserves a Gengshi-era children's song: 'Whether peace comes lies with the Red Eyebrows;

sentences.zh: 得不得，在河北。

translations.literal: gaining it or not lies with Hebei.'
translations.idiomatic: whether the throne is won lies with Hebei.'

sentences.zh: 』後更始為赤眉所殺，是不諧也；

translations.literal: Afterwards the Gengshi Emperor was killed by the Red Eyebrows—this was not harmony;
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi died at the hands of the Red Eyebrows—not harmony,

sentences.zh: 光武由河北而興，是得之也。」〉

translations.literal: Guangwu rose from Hebei—this was gaining it.'>
translations.idiomatic: while Guangwu rose from the north—gaining it, as the rhyme foretold.>

sentences.zh: 鎮慰州郡。

translations.literal: He pacified and comforted the provinces and commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: His mission was to calm and reassure every circuit he passed through.

sentences.zh: 所到部縣，輒見二千石、長吏、三老、官屬，下至佐史，

translations.literal: In every commandery and county he reached, he invariably interviewed the two-thousand-shi officials, senior magistrates, the three elders, and the whole staff down to assistants and clerks,
translations.idiomatic: Wherever he went he called in the prefects, county heads, village elders, and every clerk from senior aides down to copyists,

sentences.zh: 〈二千百謂郡守也。

translations.literal: <Two thousand shi refers to the commandery governor.
translations.idiomatic: <The two-thousand-shi grade means the commandery governor.

sentences.zh: 長吏謂縣令長及丞尉也。

translations.literal: Senior magistrates means the county magistrate or chief and the assistant and sheriff.
translations.idiomatic: By 'senior magistrates' the gloss means the county magistrate or chief, plus the assistant and sheriff.

sentences.zh: 三老者，郷官也。

translations.literal: The three elders are village officers.
translations.idiomatic: The three elders were officially recognized village headmen.

sentences.zh: 高祖置。

translations.literal: Gaozu established them.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Gaozu had instituted the office.

sentences.zh: 《前書》曰：「舉人年五十已上，有修行能帥衆者，置以為三老，每郷一人；

translations.literal: The Former Han History says: 'Select men over fifty years of age who have moral conduct and can lead the multitude; appoint them as three elders, one per village;
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu prescribes: choose men past fifty of proven character who can guide the people; name one three-elder per township;

sentences.zh: 擇郷三老為縣三老，與令長丞尉以事相敎，復其傜戍。

translations.literal: choose the village three elders to be county three elders, and they shall instruct affairs together with the magistrate or chief, assistant, and sheriff, and their labor and garrison duties shall be remitted.'
translations.idiomatic: promote a township elder to county elder, and let him counsel the magistrate, chief, assistant, and sheriff while exempting him from corvée and frontier service.'

sentences.zh: 」《續漢志》曰「每刺史皆有從事史、假佐，每縣各置諸事曹史」也。〉

translations.literal: ' The Continued Han Treatise says: 'Every regional inspector has attendant clerks and acting assistants, and each county sets up the various bureau clerks.'>
translations.idiomatic: The Xu Han zhi adds that each inspector keeps attendants and aides, and every county maintains its bureau staff.>

sentences.zh: 考察黜陟，如州牧行部事。

translations.literal: He examined and promoted or dismissed them, as when a regional shepherd makes a circuit inspection.
translations.idiomatic: He audited local government much as a regional inspector on tour would,

sentences.zh: 〈漢初遣丞相史分刺州，武帝改置刺史，察州，秩六百石。

translations.literal: <In early Han the Chancellor's clerks were sent out to supervise the provinces; Wudi changed the system and set up regional inspectors to oversee the provinces at six hundred shi salary.
translations.idiomatic: <Early Han used the Chancellor's agents to watch the circuits; Emperor Wu replaced them with regional inspectors at six hundred shi,

sentences.zh: 成帝更名牧，秩二千石。

translations.literal: Chengdi changed the title to shepherd at two thousand shi salary.
translations.idiomatic: and under Emperor Cheng the post became 'shepherd' at two thousand shi.

sentences.zh: 漢官典儀曰「刺史行郡國，省察政敎，黜陟能不，斷理冤獄」也。〉

translations.literal: The Han Official Ceremonial says: 'When the regional inspector travels through commanderies and principalities, he inspects government and instruction, promotes or demotes competence, and settles unjust imprisonment.'>
translations.idiomatic: The Han guan dian yi says inspectors on circuit review policy, advance able men, right wrongful jails, and censure the inept.>

sentences.zh: 輒平遣囚徒，除王莽苛政，

translations.literal: He regularly released prisoners and abolished Wang Mang's harsh policies,
translations.idiomatic: He emptied the jails where he could and repealed Mang's petty tyrannies,

sentences.zh: 〈《說文》曰：「苛，小草也。

translations.literal: <The Shuowen says: 'Ke is a small plant.'
translations.idiomatic: <The Shuowen glosses ke as a tiny weed—

sentences.zh: 」言政令繁細。

translations.literal: ' meaning government orders are intricate and minute.
translations.idiomatic: hence by extension 'nit-picking rule.'

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「苛政猛於虎。」〉

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: 'Harsh government is fiercer than a tiger.'>
translations.idiomatic: The Liji warns that oppressive law is worse than a tiger.>

sentences.zh: 復漢官名。

translations.literal: He restored Han official titles.
translations.idiomatic: He brought back the old Han table of ranks and titles.

sentences.zh: 吏人喜恱，爭持牛酒迎勞。

translations.literal: Officials and commoners rejoiced and vied to bring oxen and wine to welcome and refresh him.
translations.idiomatic: Clerks and townsfolk were delighted, pressing beef and wine on his escort at every stop.

sentences.zh: 進至邯鄲，

translations.literal: He advanced to Handan,
translations.idiomatic: He pressed on to Handan,

sentences.zh: 〈縣名，屬趙國，今洺州縣也。

translations.literal: <A county name, belonging to the principality of Zhao; it is modern Mingzhou county.
translations.idiomatic: <Handan county in Zhao; the town lies in present-day southern Hebei around the old Mingzhou prefecture.

sentences.zh: 《前書》音義：「邯，山名；

translations.literal: The Former Han yin yi: 'Han is a mountain name;
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu yin yi derives Han from the hill,

sentences.zh: 鄲，盡也。

translations.literal: Dan means exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: and dan as 'exhausted' or 'ended,'

sentences.zh: 邯山至此而盡。

translations.literal: The Han hill ends here.
translations.idiomatic: because the Han range stops there.

sentences.zh: 城郭字皆從邑，因以名焉。」〉

translations.literal: The graphs for city walls all take the 'settlement' signific; therefore it is named thus.'>
translations.idiomatic: City names take the 'mound' radical, whence the compound.>

sentences.zh: 故趙繆王子林

translations.literal: former Prince Miu of Zhao's son Lin
translations.idiomatic: there waited Lin, son of Prince Miu of Zhao,

sentences.zh: 〈繆王，景帝七代孫，名元。

translations.literal: <Prince Miu was the seventh-generation descendant of Jingdi, named Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: <Prince Miu was Emperor Jing's seventh-generation descendant, Yuan by name.

sentences.zh: 《前書》曰，元坐殺人，為大鴻臚所奏。

translations.literal: The Former Han History says Yuan was impeached by the Grand Herald for homicide.
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu records that Yuan was denounced to the Grand Herald for murder,

sentences.zh: 謚曰繆，音謬。

translations.literal: His posthumous title is Miu, read like 'error.'
translations.idiomatic: and received the posthumous epithet Miu, punning on 'blunder.'

sentences.zh: 《東觀記》曰「林」作「臨」字。〉

translations.literal: The Eastern Lodge Record writes the character for his name Lin as the character lin meaning 'to approach'.>
translations.idiomatic: The Dongguan ji variant spells his name with the character for 'approach' instead of 'forest'.>

sentences.zh: 說光武曰：「赤眉今在河東，但決水灌之，百萬之衆可使為魚。」

translations.literal: He urged Guangwu: 'The Red Eyebrows are now in Hedong; if you only breach the dikes and flood them, you can make a million men into fish.'"
translations.idiomatic: Lin told him: 'The Red Eyebrows are camped in Hedong—cut the levees and you will drown a hundred thousand rebels like minnows in a pond.'"

sentences.zh: 〈赤眉賊帥樊崇等恐其衆與王莽兵亂，皆朱其眉以相別，故曰赤眉。

translations.literal: <The Red Eyebrow bandit chiefs Fan Chong and the rest, fearing their followers would mingle with Wang Mang's troops in confusion, all reddened their eyebrows to tell one another apart; hence they are called Red Eyebrows.
translations.idiomatic: <Fan Chong's host dyed their brows red so as not to merge with Mang's soldiers—hence the nickname Red Eyebrows.

sentences.zh: 《續漢書》曰：「是時上平河北，過邯鄲，林進見，言赤眉可破。

translations.literal: The Continued Han History says: 'At this time His Majesty was pacifying north of the river, passed Handan, and Lin came forward to say the Red Eyebrows could be destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: The Xu Han shu narrates: while Guangwu secured Hebei and passed Handan, Lin claimed he could finish the Red Eyebrows,

sentences.zh: 上問其故，對曰：『河水從列人北流；

translations.literal: The sovereign asked the reason; he replied: 'The Yellow River flows north from Lieren;
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked how; Lin said the main channel ran north of Lieren county,

sentences.zh: 如決河水灌之，皆可令為魚。

translations.literal: if you breach the river and pour it over them, you can make them all into fish.'
translations.idiomatic: and a deliberate break would sheet the floodplain until every man swam with the carp.'

sentences.zh: 』上不然之。

translations.literal: ' The sovereign did not approve.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu dismissed the scheme.
